## Level 4 · data.table

**data.table** treats columns as memory you reference, not copy — syntax `[i, j, by]` expresses filter, compute, and group in one line. AI often mixes data.table with dplyr in one script; experienced analysts pick one dialect per workflow for readability.

Keys, binary search, and `:=` assignment by reference are performance tools, but the deeper win is explicit grouping via `by` and `.SD`. Predict row counts per group before running code — the same hypothesis-check habit from scientific reasoning practice ([Klahr & Dunbar, 1988](https://doi.org/10.1016/0166-4115(88)90004-2)).

When CSVs grow large, **`fread()`** replaces **`read.csv()`**; we note it in a quest but keep setup on `read.csv()` so renders stay portable.



## Q1 · as.data.table() mindset {#q01}

**`as.data.table()`** converts data frames without dropping columns; printing shows head and tail of large tables.

data.table is still tabular data — but `[` overloads filtering, grouping, and assignment.

Convert reef data and inspect structure.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
print(DTreef)                                     # data.table print method
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}


## Q2 · [i, j, by] core {#q02}

The template **`DT[i, j, by]`** uses **i** to filter rows, **j** to compute, **by** to group. The `.()` helper builds j expressions with names.

Read expressions aloud before running — catches AI mistakes like filtering on wrong column.

Mean count by species for North site transects only.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
DTreef[site == "North", .(mean_count = mean(count)), by = species]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
       species mean_count
        <char>      <num>
1:  Parrotfish       5.25
2: Surgeonfish      10.25
3:     Grouper       2.00
4:     Snapper       4.50
5:  Damselfish      22.00
```


:::
:::


## Q3 · .N and .I {#q03}

**.N** counts rows in the current group; **.I** holds indices for within-group positions.

These symbols replace verbose split-apply counting in base R.

Count acoustic rows per animal and pull each tag's maximum detection.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
DTac[, .N, by = animal_id]                        # rows per tag
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   animal_id     N
      <char> <int>
1:      A101     8
2:      A102     8
3:      A103     8
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
DTac[, .(max_det = max(detections)), by = animal_id]    # peak detection per tag
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   animal_id max_det
      <char>   <int>
1:      A101       7
2:      A102       5
3:      A103       7
```


:::
:::


## Q4 · .SD group columns {#q04}

**.SD** is the Subset of Data for each group — all non-group columns inside j.

Combine with `.SDcols` to apply functions across selected numeric columns.

Mean depth and pitch per biologging burst.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
DTbio[, lapply(.SD, mean), by = burst_id, .SDcols = c("depth_m", "pitch_deg")]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
    burst_id  depth_m  pitch_deg
       <int>    <num>      <num>
 1:        1 4.868771 -10.472410
 2:        2 4.429912  -4.306365
 3:        3 4.762483  -1.356978
 4:        4 5.566435  -1.278042
 5:        5 5.259435  -4.242083
 6:        6 4.977184   5.621892
 7:        7 5.124734  -3.856284
 8:        8 5.350061  -1.268552
 9:        9 5.604608   5.217801
10:       10 3.764709  -3.136650
```


:::
:::


## Q5 · := assign by reference {#q05}

**`:=`** adds or updates columns in place without copying the whole table — fast but side-effectful.

Start with simple global assignments before grouped := patterns.

Add kilograms column to fishery landings.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
DTfish[, kg := tonnes * 1000]                     # new column in place
head(DTfish[, .(species, tonnes, kg)])                  # verify conversion
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   species tonnes    kg
    <char>  <num> <num>
1: Snapper   75.7 75700
2: Grouper   79.6 79600
3:    Tuna   23.5 23500
4:   Shark   67.7 67700
5: Snapper   58.0 58000
6: Grouper   80.6 80600
```


:::
:::


## Q6 · setorder() fast sort {#q06}

**`setorder()`** sorts by reference; sorting biologging by burst and second prepares time-series ops.

Unlike dplyr arrange, setorder mutates the table — copy first if you need the original order.

Order biolog by burst_id then seconds.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
setorder(DTbio, burst_id, seconds)                # in-place sort
head(DTbio, 5)                                          # inspect first rows
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   burst_id seconds  depth_m  pitch_deg
      <int>   <int>    <num>      <num>
1:        1       0 4.798260 -27.611852
2:        1       1 5.065770 -20.483881
3:        1       2 4.755086 -17.718719
4:        1       3 4.730074  -6.960103
5:        1       4 4.665214 -24.308871
```


:::
:::


## Q7 · Keys and binary search {#q07}

A **key** indexes sorted column(s) — subsetting `DT[id]` uses binary search without merge syntax.

Set keys on animal_id for acoustic lookups.

Key acoustic table and extract animal A101.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
DTac_k <- copy(DTac)                              # avoid mutating shared object
setkey(DTac_k, animal_id)                               # sort and index
DTac_k["A101"]                                          # fast row subset
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Key: <animal_id>
   animal_id receiver detections reef_zone
      <char>   <char>      <int>    <char>
1:      A101      R11          5     Slope
2:      A101       R2          2     Crest
3:      A101       R6          7     Slope
4:      A101       R1          4     Crest
5:      A101       R5          5     Slope
6:      A101       R7          4     Slope
7:      A101       R7          4     Crest
8:      A101       R9          1     Crest
```


:::
:::


## Q8 · merge() joins {#q08}

**`merge()`** joins tables; with keys set, joins can be faster but always check nrow after.

Attach port region metadata to fishery landings.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ports <- data.table(port = c("Cairns", "Broome", "Mooloolaba"),
                      region = c("GBR", "NW", "SEQ"))
merge(DTfish, ports, by = "port", all.x = TRUE)         # keep all landings
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Key: <port>
          port  year species tonnes     kg region
        <char> <int>  <char>  <num>  <num> <char>
```

## Q9 · rbindlist() stack {#q09}

**`rbindlist()`** binds a list of data.tables quickly — stack survey legs or batches.

Use `fill = TRUE` when column sets differ slightly.

Stack North and South reef subsets.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
north <- DTreef[site == "North"]                  # subset table
south <- DTreef[site == "South"]                        # another subset
rbindlist(list(north, south), use.names = TRUE)         # combined table
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
    transect_id   site     species count depth_m
         <char> <char>      <char> <int>   <num>
 1:         T01  North  Parrotfish     4     6.2
 2:         T01  North Surgeonfish    12     6.2
```

## Q10 · dcast() wide {#q10}

**`dcast()`** pivots long to wide — detection totals by animal and receiver.

Provide fun.aggregate when duplicate keys exist.

Wide matrix of summed detections.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
dcast(DTac, animal_id ~ receiver, value.var = "detections", fun.aggregate = sum, fill = 0)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Key: <animal_id>
   animal_id    R1   R11   R12    R2    R4    R5    R6    R7    R8    R9
      <char> <int> <int> <int> <int> <int> <int> <int> <int> <int> <int>
1:      A101     4     5     0     2     0     5     7     8     0     1
2:      A102     3     3     0     8     0     0     0     4     0     3
3:      A103     0     3    14     5     7     1     0     5     3     0
```


:::
:::


## Q11 · melt() long {#q11}

**`melt()`** unpivots wide tables — inverse of dcast for export pipelines.

Set id.vars to columns that identify rows.

Melt wide 2020 landings by species columns.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
wide <- dcast(DTfish[year == 2020], year ~ species, value.var = "tonnes", fill = 0)
melt(wide, id.vars = "year", variable.name = "species", value.name = "tonnes")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
    year species tonnes
   <int>  <fctr>  <num>
1:  2020 Grouper   23.0
2:  2020   Shark   89.9
3:  2020 Snapper  144.5
4:  2020    Tuna   43.7
```


:::
:::


## Q12 · shift() like lag {#q12}

**`shift()`** moves values within groups — lag and lead for time series inside bursts.

Negative n shifts forward (lead); default n is one step lag.

Depth change between consecutive seconds per burst.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
DTbio[, depth_change := depth_m - shift(depth_m), by = burst_id]
DTbio[!is.na(depth_change), .(burst_id, seconds, depth_change)][1:8]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   burst_id seconds depth_change
      <int>   <int>        <num>
1:        1       1   0.26751027
2:        1       2  -0.31068411
3:        1       3  -0.02501225
4:        1       4  -0.06485909
5:        1       5   0.20001095
6:        1       6   0.17244104
7:        1       7   0.38211299
8:        1       8  -0.41157372
```


:::
:::


## Q13 · fcase() like case_when {#q13}

**`fcase()`** evaluates vectorised conditions top-down — fast alternative to dplyr case_when inside j.

Use default= for a catch-all bin.

Classify fishery landings into size bins.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
DTfish[, size_bin := fcase(
  tonnes < 30, "small",
  tonnes < 70, "medium",
  default = "large"
)]
DTfish[, .N, by = size_bin]                             # bin counts
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   size_bin     N
     <char> <int>
1:    large     9
2:    small     5
3:   medium    10
```


:::
:::


## Q14 · Grouped chain ; {#q14}

Curly braces with semicolons run multiple j expressions on the same filtered table — compact EDA.

Keep chains short and commented when teaching.

East reef: overall means then top species counts.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
east <- DTreef[site == "East"]
{
  east[, .(mean_depth = mean(depth_m), total_fish = sum(count))];
  east[, .(species, count)][order(-count)]
}
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
        species count
         <char> <int>
 1:  Damselfish    26
 2:  Damselfish    26
 3:  Damselfish    26
 4:  Damselfish    18
 5: Surgeonfish    14
 6: Surgeonfish    13
 7:  Parrotfish    13
 8:  Parrotfish    11
 9: Surgeonfish    10
10: Surgeonfish     9
11:     Grouper     8
12:     Snapper     8
13:     Grouper     8
14:  Parrotfish     6
15:     Snapper     5
16:  Parrotfish     5
17:     Grouper     4
18:     Grouper     4
19:     Snapper     4
20:     Snapper     1
        species count
```


:::
:::


## Q15 · fread() big CSV {#q15}

**`fread()`** reads large CSVs faster than read.csv from the same project-relative path.

If fread is unavailable, read.csv plus as.data.table still works.

Demonstrate fread on reef path with read.csv fallback.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
reef_fread <- fread(data_path("reef_transects.csv"))    # fast read; same path as setup
nrow(reef_fread)                                        # should match DTreef
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 60
```


:::
:::


## Q16 · Telemetry aggregation {#q16}

Group acoustic detections by reef_zone and receiver — standard passive telemetry summaries.

Use .() in j for named columns ready for plotting.

Sum detections and count rows per zone-receiver cell.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
tel <- DTac[, .(total = sum(detections), n = .N), by = .(reef_zone, receiver)]
tel[order(-total)][1:10]                                # top ten cells
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
    reef_zone receiver total     n
       <char>   <char> <int> <int>
 1:     Slope       R7    11     3
 2:     Crest       R2     7     2
 3:     Slope       R6     7     1
 4:     Crest      R12     7     1
 5:    Lagoon      R12     7     1
 6:     Slope       R4     7     1
 7:     Crest       R7     6     2
 8:    Lagoon      R11     6     3
 9:     Slope      R11     5     1
10:     Slope       R5     5     1
```


:::
:::


## Q17 · Fishery by year {#q17}

Annual summaries with by = year mirror agency reports — sum tonnes and count records separately.

Records and biomass answer different scientific questions.

Tonnes and landing record counts per year.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
DTfish[, .(tonnes = sum(tonnes), records = .N), by = year][order(year)]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
    year tonnes records
   <int>  <num>   <int>
1:  2018  246.5       4
2:  2019  277.1       4
3:  2020  301.1       4
4:  2021  241.6       4
5:  2022  202.5       4
6:  2023  179.8       4
```


:::
:::


## Q18 · Biologging rolling mean {#q18}

Rolling means smooth depth noise along bursts — three-point centred window using shift sums.

Production code may use frollmean; here we teach the lag-lead pattern explicitly.

Centred three-second mean depth per burst.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
DTbio[, roll_depth := (shift(depth_m, 1, type = "lag") + depth_m + shift(depth_m, 1, type = "lead")) / 3, by = burst_id]
DTbio[!is.na(roll_depth), .(burst_id, seconds, depth_m, roll_depth)][1:8]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   burst_id seconds  depth_m roll_depth
      <int>   <int>    <num>      <num>
1:        1       1 5.065770   4.873038
2:        1       2 4.755086   4.850310
3:        1       3 4.730074   4.716791
4:        1       4 4.665214   4.753504
5:        1       5 4.865225   4.856035
6:        1       6 5.037666   5.107557
7:        1       7 5.419779   5.155217
8:        1       8 5.008206   5.164646
```


:::
:::


## Q19 · .BY in j {#q19}

**.BY** exposes current by-group values inside j — handy for labels without repeating columns.

.BY is a list; index the first element for single-column groups.

Summarise detections with explicit zone label from .BY.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
DTac[, .(
  zone = .BY[[1]],
  total = sum(detections)
), by = reef_zone]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   reef_zone   zone total
      <char> <char> <int>
1:     Slope  Slope    43
2:     Crest  Crest    26
3:    Lagoon Lagoon    22
```


:::
:::


## Q20 · data.table capstone {#q20}

Capstone combines aggregation, merge, and ggplot on reef totals — meeting-ready output you verify before AI optimises.

Check nrow after merge and that grouping columns match field definitions.

Site totals joined to management metadata and plotted.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
meta <- data.table(site = c("North", "South", "East"), mgmt = c("MPA", "Open", "MPA"))
reef_sum <- DTreef[, .(total = sum(count), species_n = uniqueN(species)), by = site]
out <- merge(reef_sum, meta, by = "site")
ggplot(as.data.frame(out), aes(x = site, y = total, fill = mgmt)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(title = "Reef fish totals by site and management", y = "Sum of counts")
```
